editor_settings.title: Canon G7X Mark II vs Mark III in 2026: Which One Actually Feels Discontinued?
editor_settings.content:
Canon G7X Mark II vs Mark III in 2026: Which One Actually Feels Discontinued?

If you want the short answer, the Mark II feels more discontinued. The Mark III looks more alive.

That is not because the Mark II suddenly became a bad camera. It is because Canon's 2026 signals around the two models are not equally strong.

Why the Mark II Feels More Ghosted

The Canon G7 X Mark II is still on Canon's U.S. support pages and still listed in Canon's U.S. store. But the shop page is marked Out Of Stock, the model is much older, and Canon is not giving it fresh public love in 2026.

That creates a very specific feeling. The camera is still there, but it does not feel actively championed.

For shoppers, that looks a lot like a slow fade even when the word discontinued is not being used directly.

Why the Mark III Looks More Alive

The Mark III has stronger current-life signals.

Canon's queue page still lists it, and Canon announced a PowerShot G7 X Mark III Special Edition on February 4, 2026 for the G series 30th anniversary.

That does not happen by accident. If you wanted one simple clue about which G7 X model still has the stronger heartbeat, it is the Mark III.

What This Means in Real Shopping Terms

If you are the type of buyer who wants official reassurance, the Mark III is the safer emotional buy.

If you are the type of buyer who mostly wants the classic G7 X photo look at a better price, the Mark II can still make more sense. It just comes with more of that weird semi-discontinued energy around stock and availability.

Feature Gap Still Matters Too

This is not just a store-status conversation.

The Mark III is also the more current creator camera:

  • 4K video
  • mic input
  • more modern social-content appeal

The Mark II still wins mainly on one thing: it can be the smarter buy when you care more about photos than creator features.

That is why this comparison is messy. The Mark II feels older in the market, but it is still very easy to like as a camera.

If You Want a Mark II, Buy It Like a Bargain Hunter

If you are going Mark II in 2026, the right attitude is not panic. It is discipline.

You buy it because you want the look, the lens, the flash, and the portability without paying stupid money for scarcity theater.

[product-card:canon-powershot-g7-x-mark-ii-201mp-digital-camera-7x-zoom-95-new]


If You Want Proof the Line Still Has Life, Look at the Mark III

The Mark III is the version that best argues against the whole dead-line narrative.

It is still the model Canon is giving the clearest current signals around, and it is also the easier choice if your use case includes video, audio, and creator workflow.

[product-card:canon-powershot-g7x-mark-iii-202mp-4k-digital-camera-42x-optical-zoom-98-new]


So Which One Actually Feels Discontinued?

The Mark II, easily.

That does not mean it is officially dead. It means it looks older, thinner on store confidence, and more rumor-prone.

The Mark III is the opposite. Hard to buy, yes. But much easier to defend as a living current member of the line.

Final Verdict

If you want the G7 X model that feels least discontinued in 2026, buy the Mark III.

If you want the G7 X model that still makes sense mainly because it is charming and cheaper when found at a sane price, buy the Mark II.

So the answer is not just about specs. It is about which model still looks active and which one already feels halfway mythologized.
